title: The Alon-Jaeger-Tarsi Conjecture via Group Ring Identities
speaker: Peter Pach
abstract: The Alon-Jaeger-Tarsi conjecture states that for any finite field F of size at least 4  and any nonsingular  matrix M over F there exists a vector x such that neither x nor Mx has a 0 component. In this talk we discuss the proof of this result for primes larger than 61 and mention further applications of our method about coset covers and additive bases. Joint work with János Nagy and István Tomon.
tags: Workshop on Additive Combinatorics and Algebraic Connections

proof_stages.label: Alon–Jaeger–Tarsi statement and necessary hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the conjecture for invertible matrices over fields of size at least four, explains why fields of size two and three and singular matrices must be excluded, and recalls that infinite fields and non-prime finite fields are already known.

proof_stages.label: Network of related conjectures and strengthenings
proof_stages.summary: The speaker situates AJT among the permanent conjecture, additive-basis conjectures, nowhere-zero flow questions, and choosability variants, recording known implications and partial bounds that motivate the approach.

proof_stages.label: Large-prime theorem and group-ring lifting identity
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated—AJT for all sufficiently large primes, more generally a small-k choosability theorem—and then recast as a conjectural lifting from a vanishing group-ring identity over Fp to the corresponding integer identity.

proof_stages.label: Small arithmetic-progression sets and avoidance lemma
proof_stages.summary: The proof introduces S_k- and M_k-type subsets of Fp, establishes or cites size bounds for them, and uses a probabilistic avoidance lemma for two bases to explain the numerical condition such as S_1(p)<sqrt(p−1).

proof_stages.label: Equivalent obstruction conditions P1–P5
proof_stages.summary: Failure of AJT is translated successively into vanishing of a coordinate-product polynomial, a zero group-ring product, and a reduced-degree condition P4, so that proving P4 false under the combinatorial bounds suffices.

proof_stages.label: Inductive contradiction by deleting factors and reducing dimension
proof_stages.summary: An induction on matrix size is sketched: a key deletion lemma removes one row factor from the zero group-ring product, a suitable column is deleted to obtain a smaller nonsingular matrix, and linear independence of powers of 1−g^{e_l} forces the smaller counterexample forbidden by induction.

proof_stages.label: Further consequences and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The group-ring technique is then applied to simultaneous avoidance for several matrices, to coefficient-set versions of additive bases, and to coset-cover bounds in abelian groups, followed by questions on thresholds and improvements to the S_k/M_k estimates.

examples.name: Two-element-field counterexample
examples.note: A displayed invertible 2-by-2 matrix over F2 leaves the only nowhere-zero candidate vector with a zero coordinate in its image, forcing the exclusion of fields of size 2.

examples.name: Repeated copies of one basis as an additive basis
